SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are empirically supported: SMN is evaluated against strong baselines on two public datasets with multiple metrics and shows substantial improvements. However, there is no formal theoretical justification for key architectural choices (word-word + sequence-sequence similarity, CNN design, GRU aggregation order), and the summary indicates only limited ablation/analysis (one multi-channel variant) and no mention of statistical significance testing. Mathematical treatment is minimal but appears consistent given the architecture; overall the methodology is sound but lacks deeper analysis isolating each component and guarantees about when the model should succeed, so I deduct one point. | B(4): The paper states clear main claims and supports them with extensive experiments showing SMN substantially outperforms a broad set of baselines on two public benchmarks (Ubuntu and Douban), with hyperparameters reported and multiple evaluation metrics (Rn@k, MAP, MRR, P@1). There are no formal mathematical proofs required and the model architecture and training details appear sufficiently specified, though the candidate retrieval relies on a heuristic tf-idf step (acknowledged) and code is not provided which limits reproducibility. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and ablations (including multi-channel variants and SMN variants), and the conclusions about SMN’s effectiveness are justified, aside from modest unexplored gaps around the retrieval step and the limited benefit of the dynamic attention variant.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are empirically supported: SMN is evaluated against strong baselines on two public datasets with multiple metrics and shows substantial improvements. However, there is no formal theoretical justification for key architectural choices (word-word + sequence-sequence similarity, CNN design, GRU aggregation order), and the summary indicates only limited ablation/analysis (one multi-channel variant) and no mention of statistical significance testing. Mathematical treatment is minimal but appears consistent given the architecture; overall the methodology is sound but lacks deeper analysis isolating each component and guarantees about when the model should succeed, so I deduct one point.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear main claims and supports them with extensive experiments showing SMN substantially outperforms a broad set of baselines on two public benchmarks (Ubuntu and Douban), with hyperparameters reported and multiple evaluation metrics (Rn@k, MAP, MRR, P@1). There are no formal mathematical proofs required and the model architecture and training details appear sufficiently specified, though the candidate retrieval relies on a heuristic tf-idf step (acknowledged) and code is not provided which limits reproducibility. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and ablations (including multi-channel variants and SMN variants), and the conclusions about SMN’s effectiveness are justified, aside from modest unexplored gaps around the retrieval step and the limited benefit of the dynamic attention variant.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion; main contributions are stated early and the Sequential Matching Network architecture is described clearly. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (datasets, baselines, metrics) effectively support the narrative. A few sections—particularly the multi-channel similarity construction and the distinctions among SMN variants—require tighter, more explicit explanation to aid readers wanting to reproduce or formally reason about choices, but overall the writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion flow and states the main contributions early (SMN architecture and empirical gains on Ubuntu/Douban). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate the multi-channel matching and results. Writing quality is good overall with adequate related-work coverage, though some parts (e.g., the limited benefit of the dynamic attention variant) could use slightly clearer explanation or expanded discussion. From an engineering-readability standpoint it’s solid with only minor presentation issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion; main contributions are stated early and the Sequential Matching Network architecture is described clearly. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (datasets, baselines, metrics) effectively support the narrative. A few sections—particularly the multi-channel similarity construction and the distinctions among SMN variants—require tighter, more explicit explanation to aid readers wanting to reproduce or formally reason about choices, but overall the writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion flow and states the main contributions early (SMN architecture and empirical gains on Ubuntu/Douban). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate the multi-channel matching and results. Writing quality is good overall with adequate related-work coverage, though some parts (e.g., the limited benefit of the dynamic attention variant) could use slightly clearer explanation or expanded discussion. From an engineering-readability standpoint it’s solid with only minor presentation issues.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares against a broad, relevant set of baselines on standard datasets with appropriate metrics and that the comparisons are fair and reproducible. I dock one point because, while ablations/variants are provided and limitations are acknowledged, the paper lacks deeper analytical isolation of components (and no statistical significance testing) to fully explain and quantify the gains.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate recent baselines (single-turn and multi-turn models including MV-LSTM, Match-LSTM, Attentive-LSTM, Multi-View, DL2R, VHRED) on two standard datasets (Ubuntu and Douban) using standard metrics (Rn@k, MAP, MRR, P@1), so coverage is strong. Comparisons appear fair (same data and evaluation metrics), and the authors acknowledge when gains are small (e.g., SMNdynamic vs SMNstatic/SMNlast). However, from the summary there is limited evidence of deeper analytical isolation of each architectural component (beyond a few variants/ablation mentions) and no mention of statistical significance testing, so I penalize slightly for insufficient principled analysis of differences.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates SMN against a broad set of strong and recent baselines (single-turn and multi-turn models such as MV-LSTM, Match-LSTM, Attentive-LSTM, Multi-View, DL2R, and generation baselines like VHRED) on the two standard public datasets (Ubuntu and Douban) using standard metrics (Rn@k, MAP, MRR, P@1). Comparisons appear fair (same datasets and metrics), limitations of the retrieval step and cases where improvements are small are explicitly acknowledged, and the paper includes variant/ablation analyses (SMNlast/static/dynamic) that clarify where gains come from. Coverage of related competing approaches is adequate for practical evaluation and reproducibility-focused readers.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper composes established components into a coherent architecture that meaningfully targets multi-turn response selection, offering practical improvements and clear variants. However, it is not a radical theoretical advance but rather an engineering-oriented synthesis of prior ideas, so its novelty is moderate rather than high. I therefore assign a mid-level originality score of 3.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper composes existing building blocks (word-word similarity matrices, sequence-level GRU states, CNN-based matching, and RNN accumulation) into a pipeline tailored for multi-turn response selection. While the specific combination—multi-channel similarity matrices per utterance plus chronological RNN aggregation—constitutes a useful architectural contribution and is clearly differentiated from single-turn and some prior multi-turn baselines, it is fundamentally an incremental synthesis of known techniques (e.g., MV-LSTM-style similarity matrices and CNN/RNN aggregators) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. As an NLP researcher I find this a moderate-novelty engineering advance rather than a highly original theoretical innovation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clear, practically-motivated architecture (SMN) that departs from prior multi-turn approaches by performing per-utterance response matching via word-word and sequence-sequence similarity matrices fed to a CNN, and then chronologically accumulating these matching vectors with an RNN. This combination and the explicit per-utterance matching + accumulation is a non-trivial architectural innovation compared to prior single-turn and multi-turn baselines evaluated (Ubuntu, Douban), and the authors clearly differentiate their design and variants (SMNlast/static/dynamic). The idea is not a radical reframing of the problem (multi-turn response selection is well-studied), but it would meaningfully inform practitioners and researchers looking for improved retrieval-based multi-turn modeling, so I rate it as notable novelty rather than purely incremental.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the topic, methodology, and empirical evaluation make this paper a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue, with solid benchmarks, metrics, and comparisons. Reviewer A’s concern about limited theoretical motivation is noted but deemed a relatively minor issue given the paper’s clear practical contributions and rigorous empirical validation, so the paper deserves the highest appropriateness score.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles multi-turn response selection — a central problem in NLP and dialogue modeling — and proposes a clearly-specified architecture evaluated on standard benchmarks (Ubuntu, Douban) with appropriate metrics and strong baselines, so the topic and audience fit a top NLP/ML venue. However, from the provided summary the work emphasizes empirical architecture engineering (multi-channel similarity matrices + CNN + RNN) with limited theoretical motivation, interpretability, or formal analysis of when the inductive biases will succeed; that reduces its fit slightly for the most theory-oriented tracks at top venues. Overall it is a good fit for the venue, with modest concerns about principled justification and analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it proposes a practically-motivated architecture (SMN) tailored to multi-turn response selection and demonstrates significant empirical gains on standard public benchmarks (Ubuntu and Douban) against a broad set of baselines using community-standard metrics (Rn@k, MAP, MRR, P@1). The methodology and evaluation match expectations for the venue (detailed architecture, hyperparameters, and systematic comparisons), and the topic clearly addresses the dialogue/retrieval-based chatbot research community targeted by such conferences.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers solid empirical validation on two public benchmarks with a wide set of baselines and adequate reporting of metrics and hyperparameters, and that related work and some limitations are discussed. However, Reviewer A’s concerns about insufficient principled motivation, lack of thorough isolated ablations for key architectural choices, limited analysis of attention dynamics and failure modes, and absence of released code/reproducibility details are persuasive. Given the strong experiments but notable gaps in analysis and reproducibility, a middle-ground score of 3 reflects useful contribution but limited methodological depth and rigor.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments are reasonably broad (two public corpora, multiple retrieval and generation baselines, and standard metrics) but fall short of the principled analysis my role requires: the paper proposes coupled word-word and sequence-sequence similarity channels and a specific CNN+RNN aggregation order without strong theoretical motivation or thorough isolated ablations for each architectural choice. Ablation variants (e.g., Multi-Channel) are reported, but key components such as the relative contributions of the two similarity matrices, the CNN design, and the GRU aggregation order are not dissected with the depth I expect; attention dynamics (SMNstatic vs SMNdynamic) are noted but underanalyzed. Related work coverage is adequate and some limitations (candidate retrieval heuristic, R@k ceiling on Douban) are acknowledged, hyperparameters are given, but lack of released code and incomplete analysis of failure modes/reliability reduce reproducibility and rigor.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper provides a solid experimental validation: evaluations on two public benchmarks (Ubuntu, Douban) against a wide set of strong baselines with multiple metrics (Rn@k, MAP, MRR, P@1) and hyperparameters reported. Appropriate ablations and variants are included (SMNlast/static/dynamic, multi-channel) and limitations such as the heuristic candidate retrieval and Douban Rn@k ceiling are explicitly discussed. Related work coverage is adequate. Missing practical reproducibility aids (code release) and runtime/efficiency analysis are not mentioned, leaving some practical gaps for deployment and exact replication.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and demonstrates solid empirical gains on public benchmarks, making the approach useful and likely to influence practitioners. Concerns about limited theoretical motivation, incomplete ablation/analysis, and modest novelty temper claims of transformative impact, but the clear engineering contributions and reproducible design justify a positive impact score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—multi-turn response selection—and proposes a concrete architecture (SMN) that leverages word-word and sequence-sequence similarity matrices, CNN-based matching, and RNN accumulation; empirical gains on Ubuntu and Douban suggest usefulness. However, from a theoretical/principled standpoint the core inductive biases (why these two similarity channels and the particular aggregation order are the right choices) are not justified, key components (multi-channel design, CNN/GRU roles, attention) lack deep ablation/theoretical analysis, and some limitations (heuristic candidate retrieval, marginal benefit of dynamic attention) constrain generality. Overall, the work is a solid, practically relevant contribution likely to spur follow-up engineering and empirical extensions but is unlikely to fundamentally change how researchers conceptualize multi-turn matching without stronger formal motivation or interpretability guarantees.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper targets an important, practical problem—multi-turn response selection for retrieval-based chatbots—and proposes a clearly engineered architecture (SMN) that models utterance-response matching at multiple granularities and accumulates signals chronologically. Empirically it demonstrates significant gains over a wide set of strong baselines on two public benchmarks (Ubuntu and Douban), which makes the approach credible and likely to influence practitioners building retrieval systems. The design choices (similarity matrices + CNN + RNN accumulation) are practically motivated and reproducible (hyperparameters reported), so researchers and engineers can build on or adapt SMN for production; however, lack of mentioned code and the incremental nature of attention variants limit its transformative potential beyond this subfield.

main_claims: Proposes the Sequential Matching Network (SMN), a new architecture for multi-turn response selection in retrieval-based chatbots that first matches a response with each utterance and then accumulates matching signals.

SMN constructs word-word and sequence-sequence (via GRU hidden states) similarity matrices as multi-channel inputs to a CNN to distill matching vectors, and uses an RNN to model utterance relationships chronologically.
SMN significantly outperforms a range of baseline methods (including state-of-the-art single-turn and multi-turn models) on two public datasets (Ubuntu and Douban) across multiple evaluation metrics.
_summary.methodology: For each utterance-response pair SMN builds a word-word similarity matrix (from embeddings) and a sequence-sequence similarity matrix (from GRU hidden states), processes these multi-channel matrices with a CNN to produce matching vectors, then accumulates these vectors in chronological order using an RNN and computes a final matching score (with variants SMNlast, SMNstatic, SMNdynamic).
metrics: Recall at position k in n candidates (Rn@k)
limitations: Candidate retrieval step uses a heuristic tf-idf based expansion and is not the focus of the paper.
Rn@k scores are low on the Douban corpus because there can be multiple correct candidates for a context, which limits maximum possible Rn@k values.
SMNdynamic is only slightly better than SMNstatic and SMNlast, suggesting attention provided limited additional benefit in their experiments.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - multi-turn response selection / retrieval-based chatbots

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Analyst
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP/ML researcher with a focus on model formalization, provable properties, and principled architectures; faculty-level experience reviewing at major NLP/ML conferences.
traits: penalises papers that provide extensive empirical results but lack theoretical motivation or formal justification for core design choices (e.g., why word-word + sequence-sequence similarity is the right inductive bias);

requires explicit analysis or ablation that isolates each architectural contribution and will deduct points if key components (multi-channel similarity matrices, CNN design, GRU aggregation order) are not theoretically or empirically validated in depth;
penalises work that does not offer interpretable explanations or guarantees about when the method should be expected to work (e.g., sensitivity to context length, failure modes);
expects strong statistical testing of reported gains (e.g., significance tests, confidence intervals) and will downscore if improvements are marginal without such analysis.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the paper advances conceptual understanding and whether components are justified; treats strong empirical gains without principled backing skeptically.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Dialogue Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Experienced researcher/engineer in dialogue systems and retrieval-based chatbots with multiple deployed systems and practical evaluation expertise.
traits: rewards papers that demonstrate clear empirical improvements on established public benchmarks (will upscore substantial wins on Ubuntu and Douban);
values engineering clarity and reproducibility: will give credit if the paper provides architecture details, training recipes, and (preferably) code or exact dataset splits;

favors novel, practically-motivated architectures that improve multi-turn modeling and will positively score contributions that lead to better end-to-end retrieval performance even absent deep theoretical analysis;
likes systematic ablations and runtime/efficiency reporting and will penalise only if practical trade-offs (latency, parameter counts) are entirely missing.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic: prioritizes empirical effectiveness, reproducibility, and engineering clarity for real-world dialogue applications; more forgiving of missing theory if experiments are strong and well-documented.
